resume:
Marcus Bramley
Director of Product

Experience
Fernlake Health (digital health platform) — Director of Product, 2019-present. Lead a product group of four PMs across patient-engagement and provider-tools areas.

Clearfield Systems — Senior Product Manager, 2014-2019. Owned the scheduling product.

What I focus on
- Portfolio strategy and roadmap prioritization
- Coaching and growing PMs
- Working with engineering and design leads on delivery
- Outcome metrics over output

Education
BS, Information Systems

Selected results
- Grew patient-engagement adoption meaningfully over two years
- Reorganized the roadmap process to a quarterly outcome-based model

References available on request.
jd: Fernlake Health is hiring a Director of Product to own our patient-engagement portfolio and lead a team of senior PMs. You will set multi-quarter strategy, make hard prioritization calls across competing initiatives, and be accountable for portfolio-level outcomes (activation, retention, and clinical engagement metrics). You will coach PMs through ambiguity, partner with engineering and design directors on delivery trade-offs, and represent the portfolio to the executive team. We need a leader who can stay on the question under pressure — who answers the prioritization or trade-off actually asked rather than retreating to philosophy. You will own resourcing decisions across squads and defend why one bet won funding over another. Requirements: 8+ years in product including 3+ leading PMs, experience owning a portfolio P&L or outcome target, and a track record of difficult prioritization in a regulated or healthcare context.

history.q: You lead four PMs across patient-engagement and provider-tools. Walk me through how your portfolio is currently structured.
Tell me about the hardest prioritization call you made in the last year — two initiatives, one budget.
For that call, what was the actual decision criterion? Why did one win over the other?
Who pushed back on that decision, and how did you handle the disagreement?
Let's get concrete on the trade-off: what did the team you defunded lose, and what was the cost of that?
What outcome metric were you betting would move, and by how much?
Did it actually move? What did the data show three months later?
If the bet hadn't paid off, what was your fallback plan?
Back to the specific question: for that one prioritization call, what was the single criterion that tipped it?

candidate_last_answer: You know, this connects to something I feel really strongly about, which is the role of product leadership in healthcare more broadly. I think our whole industry has lost sight of the patient. Everyone's chasing engagement metrics, but are we actually improving outcomes? That's the question that keeps me up at night. I was at a conference last month and a clinician said something that really stuck with me about how software has changed the doctor-patient relationship, and honestly I think that's the conversation we should all be having. Prioritization frameworks are fine, but they're downstream of values.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: Prioritization frameworks are fine, but they're downstream of values

expected-yield: the single concrete decision criterion that tipped the specific two-initiative prioritization call, walking the actual sequence of how the candidate weighed them, rather than drifting into industry philosophy
